thm:1.3.summary: Bombieri–Vinogradov for nilsequence twists at level $1/4$: $\sum_{d\le x^{1/4-\varepsilon}}\max_{(c,d)=1}\sup_{\psi\in\Psi_s}|\sum_{n\le x,\,n\equiv c\,(d)}\Lambda(n)\psi(n) - \text{(main term)}|$ is small — equidistribution of $\Lambda$ twisted by a degree-$s$ nilsequence, on average over moduli.

thm:1.4.summary: Bombieri–Vinogradov for nilsequence twists at level $1/3$ (for a fixed nilsequence $\psi$), pushing the average-modulus range to $x^{1/3-\varepsilon}$.

thm:1.6.summary: Bombieri–Vinogradov for nilsequence twists at level $1/2$ with well-factorable weights $(\lambda_d)$ of level $x^{1/2-\varepsilon}$ — the well-factorable form needed for sieve applications.

cor:1.9.summary: Polynomial-phase BV at level $1/4$: the $\psi=e(P(n))$ specialization of Theorem 1.3, for $\deg P \le s$.

cor:1.10.summary: Polynomial-phase BV at level $1/3$: the $e(P(n))$ specialization of Theorem 1.4.

cor:1.11.summary: Polynomial-phase BV at level $1/2$ with well-factorable weights: the $e(P(n))$ specialization of Theorem 1.6.

thm:2.3.summary: Application: bounded gaps between primes in a nil-Bohr set $B=\{n:\|Q(n)\|<\rho\}$ for $Q$ with an irrational non-constant coefficient.

thm:2.5.summary: Application: infinitely many Chen primes $p$ (with $p+2$ prime or a product of two primes) satisfying $\|Q(p)\| < p^{-\theta_s}$ in a nil-Bohr set.

thm:2.7.summary: Application: solubility of finite-complexity systems of linear equations in primes lying in arithmetic progressions to large moduli $Q\le x^{1/3-\varepsilon}$, for almost all $q\le Q$.

thm:2.9.summary: Gowers uniformity of the primes in arithmetic progressions to large moduli $Q\le x^{1/3-\varepsilon}$: for almost all $q$, the $W$-tricked von Mangoldt $\Lambda_{a,q}$ is small in every Gowers $U^k$ norm.

cor:2.10.summary: Green–Tao theorem in progressions to large moduli: for almost all $q\le x^{1/4-\varepsilon}$, each $\mathbb{P}\cap(q\mathbb{Z}+a)$ with $(a,q)=1$ contains $k$-term arithmetic progressions.

thm:3.5.summary: Quantitative Leibman theorem: a polynomial sequence $g(n)\Gamma$ on a nilmanifold $G/\Gamma$ that is not $\delta$-equidistributed must correlate with a nontrivial horizontal character — the quantitative equidistribution dichotomy.

thm:3.7.summary: Factorization theorem: any polynomial sequence factors as smooth $\times$ equidistributed $\times$ rational on a subnilmanifold — the Green–Tao structural decomposition used to reduce to equidistributed nilsequences.

thm:4.2.summary: BV for equidistributed nilsequences at level $1/4$: the version restricted to nilsequences with a quantitative equidistribution hypothesis, which (via §4.2) implies Theorem 1.3.

thm:4.3.summary: BV for equidistributed nilsequences at level $1/3$; implies Theorem 1.4.

thm:4.4.summary: BV for equidistributed nilsequences at level $1/2$ with well-factorable weights; implies Theorem 1.6.

thm:4.6.summary: Theorems 4.2–4.4 continue to hold with $\Lambda$ replaced by the $k$-fold divisor function $d_k$ ($k\in\mathbb{C}$) or by $1_S$ for $S$ the sums of two squares — extending the equidistributed-nilsequence BV to other arithmetic weights.

prop:5.2.summary: Type I estimate: bounds the contribution of smooth (divisor-like) variables to the twisted bilinear sum, one of the two halves of the dispersion argument.

prop:6.3.summary: Type II estimate: the bilinear estimate controlling $\sum \alpha_n\beta_m \psi(nm)$ against an equidistributed nilsequence $\psi$ — the analytic heart driving the equidistributed-nilsequence BV theorems.

prop:8.2.summary: Sieve lemma packaging the well-factorable BV input into a lower-bound sieve, used to derive the bounded-gaps and Chen-prime applications.

def:1.1.summary: Nilsequences: for a connected, simply-connected nilpotent Lie group $G$ with lattice $\Gamma$ and a degree-$s$ filtration $G_\bullet$, a degree-$s$ nilsequence is $\psi(n)=F(g(n)\Gamma)$ for a polynomial sequence $g$ and Lipschitz $F$ — the higher-order-Fourier objects the BV theorem is proved against.

def:1.5.summary: Well-factorable sequences: $(\lambda_d)$ of level $D$ supported on $[1,D]$ admitting factorizations $\lambda = \alpha\star\beta$ at every intermediate scale — the weight class that lets the level reach $1/2$ in sieve applications.

prop:4.7.summary: Equidistributed-nilsequence estimate at level $1/2$: for $\varepsilon\in(0,1/2)$ there is $\kappa>0$ with the averaged-modulus bound up to $x^{1/2-\varepsilon}$ against quantitatively equidistributed nilsequences — the proposition behind the well-factorable Theorem 4.4.

prop:5.4.summary: Type I estimate: for $M\le x^{1/2}$ and $D\le x^{1/2-\varepsilon}$, bounds the smooth-variable (Type I) contribution to the twisted sum against an equidistributed nilsequence.

prop:6.5.summary: Well-factorable Type II estimate: for $M\in[x^{1/2},x^{3/4}]$ and a fixed $c\ne 0$, the bilinear estimate with well-factorable weights — the Type II input that pushes the level to $1/2$ for Theorem 4.4.

prop:6.7.summary: Type II estimate with a fixed nilsequence: for $M\in[x^{1/2},x^{2/3+\varepsilon/2}]$, the bilinear estimate for a single (non-averaged) nilsequence, feeding the level-$1/3$ Theorem 4.3.

prop:8.3.summary: Chen's sieve: for nonnegative weights $(\omega_n)$, the switching/Chen sieve producing almost-primes, the sieve engine behind the Chen-primes-in-Bohr-sets application (Theorem 2.5).

edges.note: Section 4.2 shows the equidistributed-nilsequence BV (Theorem 4.2) implies the general level-$1/4$ BV (Theorem 1.3).
Section 4.3 shows Theorem 4.3 implies Theorem 1.4.
Section 4.3 shows Theorem 4.4 implies Theorem 1.6.
The polynomial-phase corollary is the $\psi=e(P(n))$ special case of the nilsequence theorem 1.3.
Polynomial-phase special case of Theorem 1.4.
Polynomial-phase special case of Theorem 1.6.
The reduction to equidistributed nilsequences uses the factorization theorem (3.7).
Quantitative equidistribution (Leibman, 3.5) underlies the equidistributed-nilsequence estimate.
The level-$1/4$ equidistributed BV is driven by the Type II estimate (6.3).
Theorem 4.2 also uses the Type I estimate (5.2).
Theorem 4.3 uses the Type II estimate (6.3).
Theorem 4.4 uses the Type II estimate (6.3).
Extends the equidistributed-nilsequence BV to the divisor functions $d_k$ and to sums of two squares.
Bounded gaps in nil-Bohr sets uses the well-factorable level-$1/2$ BV (Theorem 1.6).
The bounded-gaps application runs the BV input through the sieve lemma (8.2).
Chen primes in nil-Bohr sets uses the well-factorable level-$1/2$ BV.
The Chen-prime application uses the sieve lemma (8.2).
Linear equations in primes in APs to moduli $\le x^{1/3}$ uses the polynomial-phase level-$1/3$ BV (1.10).
Gowers uniformity of primes in APs to large moduli uses the level-$1/3$ polynomial-phase BV (1.10).
The Green–Tao theorem in progressions follows from Gowers uniformity of the primes in those progressions (2.9).
The BV theorem is stated for twists by degree-$s$ nilsequences (Definition 1.1).
The level-$1/2$ theorem uses well-factorable weights (Definition 1.5).
The level-$1/2$ equidistributed BV (Theorem 4.4) is packaged from the equidistributed-nilsequence estimate (Proposition 4.7).
Theorem 4.4 is driven by the well-factorable Type II estimate (Proposition 6.5).
Theorem 4.3 uses the fixed-nilsequence Type II estimate (Proposition 6.7).
Theorem 4.2 uses the Type I estimate (Proposition 5.4) alongside Proposition 5.2.
The level-$1/2$ equidistributed estimate rests on the well-factorable Type II estimate (Proposition 6.5).
The well-factorable Type II estimate reduces to the equidistributed case via the factorization theorem (3.7).
Chen primes in nil-Bohr sets use Chen's sieve (Proposition 8.3).

main_BV_theorems.description: Bombieri–Vinogradov for nilsequence twists at levels $1/4$, $1/3$, and $1/2$ (well-factorable).

polynomial_phase.description: The $\psi=e(P(n))$ specializations of the main theorems.

applications.description: Downstream applications: bounded gaps and Chen primes in nil-Bohr sets, linear equations and Gowers uniformity of primes in APs to large moduli, and Green–Tao in progressions.

equidistribution_tools.description: The Green–Tao nilsequence machinery: quantitative Leibman equidistribution and the factorization theorem.

definitions.description: The two objects the theorem is built around: nilsequences and well-factorable weight sequences.

equidistributed_BV.description: BV restricted to equidistributed nilsequences at each level, which imply the general theorems, plus the level-$1/2$ proposition and the extension to other arithmetic weights.

type_I_II.description: The Type I and Type II bilinear estimates driving the equidistributed-nilsequence BV, including the well-factorable and fixed-nilsequence variants.

sieve.description: The lower-bound sieve and Chen's sieve converting the BV input into the prime-gap and Chen-prime applications.

GreenTaoNilsequences.title: Green–Tao — The quantitative behaviour of polynomial orbits on nilmanifolds

GreenTaoZiegler.title: Green–Tao–Ziegler — The inverse conjecture for the Gowers norms

BFI.title: Bombieri–Friedlander–Iwaniec — Primes in arithmetic progressions to large moduli

Zhang.title: Y. Zhang — Bounded gaps between primes
